quote: They never called me on the night that they were supposed to call, and then they called me, like, two days later.
stewart_read: Gary is not venting — he is delivering a specific, factual account of a service failure. This is the highest-leverage moment in the call. A trust-repair move here ('Here's what I'm doing right now to make sure Monday is locked — I'm putting a note directly on the specialist's calendar and you'll get a confirmation text from me today') converts the grievance into a commitment signal. Meg's response is a quick apology followed by an immediate pivot to scheduling. The apology is genuine but the pivot is too fast — she resolves the logistics without resolving the trust.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg moved quickly past the grievance to avoid dwelling on Ion's failure and keep Gary's attention on the rebook. Rejected — there is no evidence of a later trust-repair move that would justify the early pivot. The grievance is simply left unresolved.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, play the clip at 00:45 and ask Meg: 'What did Gary need to hear before he'd trust us to show up on Monday?' The answer is not an apology — it is a specific action Meg is taking right now to make Monday different. Coach her to name one concrete step (confirmation text, note on the specialist's file) before moving to scheduling.

quote: You said next week is best for you. Right?
stewart_read: Meg answers her own confirmation question and accepts her own 'Yeah' as Gary's commitment. This is a leading close, not a genuine confirmation. Gary's actual words at 01:38 ('That'll work') are transactionally cooperative but not enthusiastic — he is going along, not committing. The booking is real but the verbal handshake that predicts show rate was never earned.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Meg is rushing to close before Gary's 'busy right now' energy causes him to disengage. Plausible, but the self-answered confirmation is still a coaching gap regardless of intent — it substitutes Meg's assumption for Gary's actual commitment.
coaching_implication: Show Meg the transcript side-by-side: her question, her own 'Yeah,' and Gary's eventual 'That'll work.' Ask her what the difference is between those two responses. The goal is for her to hear that she closed on her own word, not his. The fix is simple: pause after the question and wait for Gary to answer.

quote: Is this the best number to call? Or I see we also have your fiance's number as well. No. This number would be best.
stewart_read: Meg surfaces the fiancée's number from the file — a positive signal that she checked the record. But she accepts Gary's 'No. This number would be best' without asking whether the fiancée should be on the appointment. The codex is explicit: a co-decision-maker on the title must be included in the appointment, not just excluded from the callback number. Meg conflates 'which number to call' with 'who should be present,' and Gary's answer to the first question closes the second without it being asked.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg may have judged that pushing for fiancée inclusion would risk the rebook on a call where Gary is already impatient. Rejected — the codex treats spouse/co-decision-maker inclusion as a protocol, not an objection to overcome. The correct move is to ask the inclusion question separately from the callback-number question.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, separate the two questions for Meg: 'Which number should we call?' is logistics. 'Should your fiancée be on the call with you?' is protocol. She asked the first and skipped the second. If the fiancée is on the title and not on the appointment, the closer walks into a structurally incomplete close. Coach Meg to ask both questions every time, regardless of the customer's energy.

quote: Just make sure you have access to your email at that time as well since we'll send you some details that way.
stewart_read: Meg's button-up substitutes an email notification for the codex-required text tieback and skips three required elements: the computer anchor ('you'll be able to sit down to review the design on your computer'), the no-driving instruction, and the 'is that fair' verbal handshake. The email mention is a partial substitute for the text follow-up but does not fulfill the personalized tieback requirement. On a recycled lead where the prior appointment was already missed, a complete button-up is the primary tool for reducing no-show risk — and it was not executed.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have judged that a full button-up would feel over-engineered on a short rebook call. Rejected — the button-up elements exist precisely because they predict show rate, and this appointment carries elevated no-show risk from the prior service failure.
coaching_implication: Walk Meg through the button-up checklist against this call: appointment datetime (yes), one-hour block stated (no), phone appointment specified (partial), no-driving/at-computer anchor (no), 'is that fair' handshake (no), text follow-up with tieback (no — email substituted, no tieback). Four of six elements missing on a fragile rebook. The coaching point is not 'follow the script' — it is 'the elements you skipped are the ones that predict whether Gary shows up Monday.'
